# The Consulting Conundrum: When Cheap Answers Meet Expensive Problems

The collapse in the cost of generating plausible answers — driven by large language models and the broader infrastructure of generative AI — is restructuring the economics of consulting in ways that create two distinct traps for clients. Both traps stem from the same underlying shift: answers that once required expensive human expertise to produce can now be generated quickly, fluently, and at negligible marginal cost. The danger is not that these answers are wrong in obvious ways but that they are plausible enough to foreclose further inquiry.

### The commodity trap

In the first trap, clients treat answers as interchangeable commodities and evaluate them primarily on cost. If a competent-sounding analysis can be produced cheaply, why pay a premium? The logic is intuitive but corrosive. As the price of answers falls, so does the likelihood that anyone involved has done the difficult, unglamorous work of interrogating the problem itself — testing assumptions, reframing the question, identifying what remains genuinely uncertain. The client receives output that reads well and costs little but that addresses the wrong problem or addresses the right problem superficially. The savings are real; the value is not.

This is a race to the bottom disguised as efficiency. When every firm can generate polished deliverables at speed, the differentiator cannot be the deliverable itself. Yet the commodity framing makes it nearly impossible for clients to see what they are missing, because what they are missing is not a better answer but a better question — and the absence of a good question is invisible in a way that the absence of a good answer is not.

### The confidence trap

In the second trap, clients do pay a premium, but they pay for the wrong signal. Consulting has always involved an element of performance — the structured presentation, the authoritative tone, the appearance of mastery. In a world where generating confident, well-organized analysis is trivially easy, these signals become even less reliable as indicators of underlying quality. A firm that invests heavily in framing the right problem and a firm that dresses up a superficial analysis in polished rhetoric can look remarkably similar from the client's perspective.

This is the problem Richard Rumelt identified as bad strategy: the substitution of ambitious-sounding goals and confident language for the hard analytical work of diagnosing the actual situation and identifying the crux of the challenge. Generative AI makes bad strategy easier to produce and harder to detect. The client who cannot evaluate the quality of the questions being asked — who judges consulting output by its fluency rather than by the structure of the inquiry that produced it — has no reliable mechanism for distinguishing genuine insight from expensive plausibility.

### The shifting bottleneck

What connects both traps is a migration in where the bottleneck sits. Historically, the consulting value chain was constrained at the point of generating competent analysis. Research was slow, synthesis was labor-intensive, and the ability to produce a well-reasoned recommendation was genuinely scarce. That scarcity justified the cost. Now that the generation of analysis is cheap and fast, the constraint moves upstream — to problem definition, to the identification of what is actually uncertain, to the judgment required to distinguish a question worth answering from one that merely feels productive.

This is the same structural pattern visible whenever a technology commoditizes a previously scarce output. Photography did not destroy painting; it shifted the locus of artistic value from representational accuracy to interpretive vision. AI coding agents did not eliminate software engineering; they migrated the engineer's core contribution from writing code to planning, orchestration, and quality judgment. In consulting, the parallel is precise: when producing answers becomes cheap, the scarce and valuable activity is the disciplined framing of problems.

### What clients need to learn

The implication for clients is uncomfortable. Evaluating consulting by the quality of its questions rather than the polish of its answers requires a kind of literacy that most organizations have not developed. It means asking not "Does this recommendation sound right?" but "What assumptions does this rest on, and how were they tested?" It means valuing the consultant who says "We're solving the wrong problem" over the one who delivers a beautifully formatted solution to the problem as initially stated. It means recognizing that the most dangerous consulting engagement is the one that feels smooth and conclusive from start to finish, because genuine uncertainty rarely resolves that cleanly.

The most expensive mistake in a world of cheap answers is not overpaying for good thinking. It is confusing cheap answers with good ones — and never realizing the difference until the strategy fails.

## See Also

- [[z20260315-018|LLMs, Cheap Answers, and the Economics of Questions]] — grounds the information-theoretic framework underlying the consulting conundrum, establishing why plausible answers carry little value when they fail to resolve meaningful uncertainty
- [[z20260315-016|The Impact of Photography on Art and Its Parallel to AI]] — exemplifies the same structural pattern in a different domain, where commoditization of a scarce output shifted value to a higher-order scarcity
- [[z20260315-008|The Transformation of the Software Engineering Role in the Age of AI Coding Agents]] — applies the questions-over-answers dynamic to software engineering, where the migration from execution to planning and judgment parallels the shift described here for consulting
